item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's kinda my theory. It has to contain nothing. nothing. So that's why it changes everything. To contain nothing, you must let everything out.

item.title: HUH
item.content:encoded:
read the comments before reading the whole scp (for some reason??) and still got knocked out of alignment by the D-class walking through the wall

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So its like SCP 055 but instead of forgetting anything related to it, anything not related to it stops making sense if I understand right? Either way a good narrative SCP. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excuse me what the fuck
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think someone broke the concept of normal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Warning: Normality has broken

… wait, is one of the Carès still in the interview room?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, thanks for catching that! Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"…and mnestic therapy of numerous personnel proved that no memories existed of the ADC's arrival."

Did you mean to say ACC, or was it meant to say ADC?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Absolutely no idea what I'm looking at. Granted that's the point, but what in the hel are you supposed to do with this?

+1 for making that poor sap take as many notes as I did in high school Precalc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
oh god this is horrifying.

Jesus Christ

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Almost feels like 3125, but had some other fuckery happening here. I have no idea what it is but I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: why do i feel like this has a relation to 4000?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(Dr. Carè takes notes for six-hundred and thirty-four years.)

it was roughly here that I realized something unusual was going on.

Anyway this one isn't my favorite on the site or anything but it's a nice, solid piece of "what the fuck was that"-ery, +1 it gets.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The title perfectly encapsulates why I like this SCP. Vagueness in horror can be so unnerving when done right, both hinting at something while saying nothing at the same time, but it's also very easy to screw up. I think you hit the right spot with this one, balancing between ridiculous and creepy and staying in that sweet spot without going too much into either direction. The article being relatively short helps a lot with this.

Easy +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 0/10 would not open

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm in love with this article, the fact that the anomaly (or malfunctioning containment chamber) dissolves reality to such a degree that even memory and documentation of it get corrupted is very well done. The whole thing reminds me of reality as a video game that glitches out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It didn't work for my taste, but that's a subjective thing.

To me, it just came across as a load of silly "LoL Foundation purple monkey dishwasher Lol-so-random hijinx". I didn't see even the remotest narrative connection between anything in the fragmented mess, and it just left me with a feeling of annoyance for Dr. Care' having been hit with the idiot ball for going into the chamber. It just wasn't believable to me that Dr. Care' would risk going in. It wasn't believable to me that the guards posted at the entrance would leave their post to assist somewhere else during a breach. That seems like LoL Foundation to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Consider a containment chamber, designed to adapt to contain any possible anomaly.

Now, put nothing in it, and let it adapt to contain that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i do not think they should have opened it. it was a bad idea

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interview logs for this are absolutely amazing. The progression between them from relatively mundane (if a little strange and humorous) all the way to that wonderfully creepy last one is excellent, and the fact that you nailed the pacing with just three logs is remarkable. A very eager +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reads very much like something the Counterconceptual Division would look over. It's got a feeling of inherent wrongness like SCP-3999 and SCP-2740. It's got the same amount of mindfuckery as 3942.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is "crazy dialogue" at its best. Extremely unnerving.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one made my brain itch uncomfortably. I really like it.

+ Here's my theory.
– hide block

My understanding of this is that the Adaptive Containment Chamber is exactly what it sounds like, something Thaumiel-ish that some Foundation built - probably through anomalous means - that can adapt itself in order to successfully secure and contain anything placed inside of it.

One day, the Foundation decided to put something very powerful in the ACC, probably as some manner of last resort, or to test the upper limits of their creation. But the ACC, as it turns out, has no upper limits, and whatever was put inside of it couldn't be properly contained without bending time and space and actively altering reality to accommodate it. So… that's exactly what it did.

And it worked! Kind of. Whatever's inside is being successfully contained, but the ACC has had to stretch itself into a very weirdly-shaped box that spans time and space (and doesn't play well with the rest of baseline reality) in order to do so. Anyone who enters the ACC will become stretched through time and space in the same manner, and when they come out… they don't snap back to their original 'shape' correctly, which is why some D-Class walk through walls, others have adverse effects on the flow of time around them, and Dr. Carè ended up becoming spatially and temporally incomprehensible.

I'm likely wrong. Nevertheless, I greatly enjoyed reading this. I love pieces like this that are incredibly bizarre, but make just enough sense for me to piece together my own weird little puzzle. :) +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are bits and pieces of this that feel rather familiar (SCP shows up in containment out of nowhere! Language fuckery! Undone by our thirst for knowledge!) but in the end I felt like it added up to a satisfying and immensely enjoyable whole.

Also, the "sick" exchange is probably my favorite offhand D-Class exchange ever.

Also also, welcome back, we missed you! My favorite time of year is when you leave your tanhony hole to grace us with your malfeasance. One more article and we get six more weeks of winter!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok, could someone explain what's going on in the article? I was very confused.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm still not sure this will survive but I do like it so I'm giving you an up vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what the fuck

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's up! Yay! I loved this when criting it, it's just such a great concept. The last interview had me hooked the whole way through!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Deliciously bizarre. My every concern was laid to rest as I proceeded through and I was honk this is the trippiest thing I’ve read here in awhile. +1

Have to mention excellent specific containment procedures that tee up the rest of the article perfectly.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded: Thanks to TheBlueHour, Crashington, TheMightyMcB, Mew-ltiverse, RadioactiveRADs and Prior for their feedback!
